original_hypothesis.title: Missing Cumulative Reserve Overflow Check in mint() and executeFeeSwap() Unlike rebalanceSwap()
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: _requireU128 validates that individual amounts fit in uint128, but mint() and executeFeeSwap() lack the explicit cumulative overflow check that rebalanceSwap() has at lines 134-136: `if (uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max) revert InsufficientReserves()`. In executeFeeSwap, `pool.reserveUserToken += uint128(amountIn)` can overflow uint128 if current reserve + amountIn exceeds the max, causing a Solidity Panic(0x11) instead of a descriptive InsufficientReserves error. In mint, `pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountValidatorToken)` has the same gap. This inconsistency is a demonstrable code defect: the developer clearly intended cumulative overflow checks (evidenced by rebalanceSwap) but omitted them in two other functions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic prevents silent overflow, the Panic revert is unrecoverable and provides no useful error information. More critically, if a future optimizer or compiler change affects checked arithmetic behavior, or if the contract is used as a library target for delegatecall from unchecked contexts, the missing guard becomes exploitable. The inconsistency also suggests the developer may have missed other checks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _requireU128 checks individual amount fits uint128; rebalanceSwap has cumulative check at line 134 `if (uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max)`; executeFeeSwap line 110 `pool.reserveUserToken += uint128(amountIn)` and mint line 199 `pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountValidatorToken)` both lack equivalent cumulative checks

preconditions: Reserve of the target token type is already close to uint128 max (~3.4e38)
A deposit or swap pushes the cumulative reserve past uint128 max

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rebalanceSwap: explicit check `uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max` at line 134. executeFeeSwap and mint: no equivalent check before `+= uint128(amount)`
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Code inconsistency: identical operation handled differently across functions. If conditions ever allow near-max reserves, executeFeeSwap and mint produce opaque Panic reverts while rebalanceSwap gives descriptive errors.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Primarily a code correctness defect; could enable DoS by pushing reserves to near-max through repeated deposits in low-decimal tokens

economic_questions: Can reserves practically approach uint128 max for any supported token?
Does the inconsistency create differential behavior exploitable by integrating contracts?
Are there tokens with sufficiently low decimals to make near-max reserves feasible?
validation_requirements: Confirm Panic revert occurs in executeFeeSwap/mint when cumulative exceeds uint128 max
Verify rebalanceSwap correctly handles the same condition with InsufficientReserves
Test with low-decimal tokens (0-2 decimals) to assess practical reachability

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Impractical for 18-decimal tokens; potentially feasible for 0-2 decimal tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Multiple large deposits to push reserves near max
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: DoS value; no direct profit extraction
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - code defect, not direct profit exploit

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Opaque reverts could confuse integrating protocols

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users and integrating protocols encountering unexpected Panic reverts

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: uint128 max = 3.4e38; for 6-decimal tokens = 3.4e32 tokens; for 18-decimal tokens = 3.4e20 tokens
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Side-by-side comparison of rebalanceSwap line 134-136 vs executeFeeSwap line 110 vs mint line 199

validation_result.impact_summary: executeFeeSwap() and mint() lack the explicit cumulative reserve overflow check present in rebalanceSwap(), causing opaque Panic(0x11) reverts instead of descriptive InsufficientReserves errors when reserves approach uint128 max.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies an inconsistency in overflow handling across three functions that perform reserve addition operations. rebalanceSwap explicitly checks `uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max` before the addition at lines 134-136, reverting with a descriptive `InsufficientReserves` error. In contrast, executeFeeSwap at line 110 performs `pool.reserveUserToken += uint128(amountIn)` and mint at line 199 performs `pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountValidatorToken)` without equivalent cumulative checks. While _requireU128 validates that the individual input amounts fit in uint128, it does not validate that the sum of the current reserve plus the new amount fits in uint128. In Solidity 0.8.28 (as specified in foundry.toml), the checked arithmetic will catch this overflow with a Panic(0x11), but this is an opaque error that provides no diagnostic value to callers or integrating contracts. The developer clearly intended to handle this case with a custom error, as demonstrated by the rebalanceSwap implementation. This is a demonstrable code defect: a missing guard that the developer intended to include based on the pattern in the sibling function.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Three code locations confirm the inconsistency: (1) rebalanceSwap lines 134-136 have the explicit cumulative check. (2) executeFeeSwap line 110 lacks it for reserveUserToken. (3) mint line 199 lacks it for reserveValidatorToken. The _requireU128 calls at lines 104-105 (executeFeeSwap) and line 195 (mint) only check individual amounts, not cumulative sums.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This is the CORRECT implementation: explicit cumulative overflow check with descriptive error. This pattern is missing from executeFeeSwap and mint.

Missing cumulative check: `uint256(pool.reserveUserToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max` is never verified. _requireU128(amountIn) only ensures amountIn alone fits in uint128, not the sum. If pool.reserveUserToken is near uint128 max and amountIn is added, the uint128 addition will Panic(0x11) instead of reverting with InsufficientReserves.
Missing cumulative check: `uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountValidatorToken > type(uint128).max` is never verified. Same defect pattern as executeFeeSwap.

calling_sequence: 3. _requireU128(amountIn) passes - amountIn fits in uint128 individually
4. _requireU128(amountOut) passes - amountOut fits in uint128 individually
5. Reserve sufficiency check passes: pool.reserveValidatorToken >= amountOut
8. If pool.reserveUserToken was 2^128 - 100 and amountIn is 200, Panic(0x11) occurs
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path through executeFeeSwap reaches line 110 without any check that `uint256(pool.reserveUserToken) + amountIn <= type(uint128).max`. The _requireU128 at line 104 only validates amountIn in isolation. The addition at line 110 relies solely on Solidity's checked arithmetic to prevent overflow, producing an opaque Panic instead of the developer-intended InsufficientReserves custom error. The identical pattern exists in mint at line 199 for reserveValidatorToken.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: foundry.toml confirms solc = 0.8.28 which has checked arithmetic, so silent overflow is prevented. However the defect is the missing explicit check and inconsistent error handling, not silent overflow.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario requires reserves approaching uint128 max (~3.4e38). For 18-decimal tokens this is ~3.4e20 tokens which is impractical for most stablecoins. For 0-2 decimal tokens, the threshold is more reachable (3.4e38 or 3.4e36 tokens respectively), though still extremely large. The defect is confirmed as a code inconsistency regardless of practical exploitability - the rebalanceSwap function demonstrates the developer's intent to handle this case explicitly.
validation_result.impact_assessment: LOW to MEDIUM severity code defect. The practical impact is primarily: (1) inconsistent error handling across functions performing identical operations, (2) opaque Panic(0x11) revert instead of descriptive InsufficientReserves in executeFeeSwap and mint, (3) potential confusion for integrating contracts that catch custom errors but not Panic codes, (4) demonstrates a pattern of incomplete safety checks that may indicate other missed validations. No direct fund loss is possible because Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic prevents the actual overflow.
recommended_mitigations: Add cumulative overflow check in executeFeeSwap before line 110: `if (uint256(pool.reserveUserToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max) revert InsufficientReserves();`
Add cumulative overflow check in mint before line 199: `if (uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountValidatorToken > type(uint128).max) revert InsufficientReserves();`

Consider extracting a shared helper function like `_safeAddReserve(uint128 current, uint256 amount) internal pure returns (uint128)` that performs both the individual and cumulative checks consistently
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy FeeAMM with a mock ERC20 with 0 decimals. 2. Mint a pool with near-max uint128 reserveValidatorToken. 3. Call executeFeeSwap to add amountIn that would push reserveUserToken past uint128 max. 4. Verify Panic(0x11) occurs instead of InsufficientReserves. 5. Compare with rebalanceSwap which should return InsufficientReserves under similar conditions.
uncertainties: Whether any realistic token deployment could cause reserves to approach uint128 max
Whether integrating contracts depend on specific custom error types for error handling
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz testing with various reserve values approaching uint128 max to confirm differential behavior between rebalanceSwap and executeFeeSwap/mint
Test with 0-decimal tokens to assess practical reachability of the overflow condition
Integration testing to verify if downstream contracts handle Panic(0x11) differently from custom errors
